key_points: Constructing a parallel line uses the property of 'Equal Alternate Interior Angles'
SSS Criterion: Constructing a triangle when all three side lengths are known
SAS Criterion: Constructing a triangle with two sides and the angle between them
Regular tilings use only one type of regular polygon (triangle, square, or hexagon)
examples: Parallel Construction: Drawing a line parallel to AB through an external point P
SSS Triangle: Sides 3 cm, 4 cm, and 5 cm
SAS Triangle: Sides 5 cm, 4 cm and an included angle of $60^{\circ}$
Hexagonal Tiling: A natural honeycomb pattern
formulas: Triangle Inequality $ightarrow a + b > c$ (Construction is impossible if this fails)
Interior angle of a regular n-gon $ightarrow  rac{(n-2) 	imes 180^{\circ}}{n}$
exam_tips: For SSS, use the longest side as the base for a more stable drawing
For SAS, construct the angle first at one endpoint before marking the second side
Label all vertices (A, B, C) and dimensions on your final construction
In tilings, check if the interior angle of the polygon is a factor of $360^{\circ}$
